verbatim_text: And when you have interactions with real users. And how to leverage feedbacks that the robots observe from the real world or from the real users. In the end, I believe that AI can be the Andrew Wiles of the world's most important and hardest problems. Okay. That's all. Thank you. Okay. I think we have time for actually a few questions. While people are thinking I'll start as you say. I think one of the big questions, I think, is this issue of dense reward, especially when you talk about discovery. Andrew Wiles probably was not testing every single thing he did against the math class theorem. He had, as you're saying, insights about what directions are good. Do you have any ideas about how to get AI models to have similar insights, maybe learn this over test time? And to add to your point, even if Andrew Wiles could test every potential proof he comes up with against the theorem, he wouldn't know.
